.aiwg/architecture/adr-001.md.title: ADR-001: Daemon Architecture over In-Process Browser
.aiwg/architecture/adr-001.md.summary: **Status**: Accepted **Date**: 2026-04-02 **Deciders**: roctinam The automation layer needs to maintain long-lived browser sessions that survive client script restarts -- for example, staying logged into a site across multiple agent invocations without re-authenticating. A naive approach of spawning a new Carbonyl process per script invocation loses all in-memory browser state (DOM, WebSocket connections, pending XHRs) on every exit, forcing full page reloads and re-authentication. The architect

.aiwg/architecture/adr-002.md.title: ADR-002: pyte Virtual Terminal for Screen Parsing
.aiwg/architecture/adr-002.md.summary: **Status**: Accepted **Date**: 2026-04-02 **Deciders**: roctinam The automation layer must extract structured text and clickable coordinates from Carbonyl's rendered output. Carbonyl renders web pages as terminal characters over a PTY, emitting raw ANSI escape sequences. The layer needs a reliable, addressable character grid that an LLM agent can reason about using text coordinates rather than pixel positions. Use pyte, a Python VT100 terminal emulator library, to maintain a `Screen(220, 50)` bu

.aiwg/architecture/adr-003.md.title: ADR-003: Firefox UA Spoofing and HTTP/2 Disabling for Bot-Detection Bypass
.aiwg/architecture/adr-003.md.summary: **Status**: Accepted **Date**: 2026-04-02 **Deciders**: roctinam Target websites (primarily USPS.com behind Akamai Bot Manager) use a multi-signal detection chain to classify automated browsers. Akamai correlates at least three signals: (1) the JA3 TLS fingerprint, which identifies the TLS library; (2) the HTTP/2 SETTINGS frame, which has Chromium-specific window sizes and header table parameters; and (3) the User-Agent string. When all three signals indicate Chromium but the UA claims to be Fir

.aiwg/architecture/adr-004.md.title: ADR-004: Chromium User-Data-Dir for Session Persistence
.aiwg/architecture/adr-004.md.summary: **Status**: Accepted **Date**: 2026-04-02 **Deciders**: roctinam The automation layer must persist browser sessions (cookies, localStorage, IndexedDB, cached credentials) across process restarts so that authenticated workflows do not require re-login on every invocation. Multiple independent sessions must coexist -- for example, separate USPS accounts or different states of the same account. The persistence mechanism must integrate with Chromium's own session management rather than fighting it.

.aiwg/reports/construction-ready-brief.m.title: Construction Ready Brief — Carbonyl Automation Layer
.aiwg/reports/construction-ready-brief.m.summary: **Project**: Carbonyl Automation Layer **Date**: 2026-04-03 **Status**: CONSTRUCTION READY --- The Carbonyl Automation Layer is a Python library (~1,720 LoC) that wraps the Carbonyl terminal browser (headless Chromium) to enable programmatic web interaction for LLM agents. The library provides a persistent daemon architecture over a Unix socket, a pyte-based virtual terminal screen inspector, and a Chromium session manager with fork/snapshot/restore semantics.

.aiwg/working/architecture/sad-review-se.title: Security Review — Carbonyl Automation Layer SAD
.aiwg/working/architecture/sad-review-se.summary: **Reviewer**: Security Architect **Date**: 2026-04-02 **Document Reviewed**: software-architecture-doc.md v1.0 **Verdict**: CONDITIONAL The SAD accurately describes the system's architecture but treats security as a description of existing behavior rather than a set of controls to be enforced. Section 8 acknowledges the `--no-sandbox` flag and default socket permissions, but does not prescribe mitigations, leaving three Critical/High risks from the risk register (R-001, R-003, R-005) unaddressed

.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/00-visi.title: Vision — Carbonyl Trusted Automation
.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/00-visi.summary: Carbonyl today can render modern SPAs in a terminal, but cannot *drive* them reliably. On React-controlled forms like `x.com/i/flow/login`, typed text is rendered into the DOM input but never propagates to React state, because synthetic PTY-derived events fail `event.isTrusted` checks. Even when `isTrusted` passes, modern bot-scoring systems (Akamai, DataDome, PerimeterX, Cloudflare) score sessions against ~50 behavioral and environmental features; failing any of them reduces Carbonyl to a toy f

.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/03-thre.title: Threat Model — Carbonyl Trusted Automation
.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/03-thre.summary:
Unusual inversion: the "adversary" here is the **bot-detection system**, not a malicious actor attempting to harm users. This threat model enumerates detection signals that distinguish Carbonyl from a human-driven Chrome, treats each as an attack vector on our goal, and maps mitigations. A parallel standard security threat model (STRIDE on Carbonyl as a deployed service) exists separately and is not in scope here. ```mermaid flowchart TB L1[Layer 1: Event trust
isTrusted flag]

.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/04-test.summary: Each detection layer is testable independently, with a concrete pass/fail signal. No requirement may be marked complete without its acceptance test passing against the corpus in `carbonyl-agent-qa`. ```         ┌─────────────────────────┐         │  E2E reference sites    │  ← x.com, Turnstile, DataDome demo         ├─────────────────────────┤

.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/07-fing.title: Fingerprint Registry — Design Document
.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/07-fing.summary: An **owned, coherent fingerprint management system** shared across `carbonyl`, `carbonyl-agent`, and `carbonyl-fleet`. Replaces the earlier "Phase 3 deferred TLS impersonation" framing with an affirmative architecture where every outbound network signal, every JS-observable surface, and every behavioural parameter is sourced from a single registry and must pass a consistency check before use. Scope: this document specifies the component boundaries, data model, APIs, and validation rules. Detaile

This doc covers the **initiative-specific** CI surface across four repos. Initiative CI must cover: 1. Per-phase test suite execution (Layers 1–4+ as they come online) 2. Cross-repo coordination — QA waits for matching Carbonyl runtime + agent build 3. `jmagly/wreq` fork lifecycle — sync, build, tag, security scan

.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/README..title: Trusted Automation Initiative — Document Corpus
.aiwg/working/trusted-automation/README..summary: **Goal:** Make Carbonyl a credible headless browser for personal automation agents against modern bot-detection stacks (Akamai Bot Manager, DataDome, PerimeterX/HUMAN, Cloudflare). **Scope:** Multi-repo initiative spanning `carbonyl` (core), `carbonyl-agent` (SDK + behavioral), `carbonyl-agent-qa` (test harness), and `carbonyl-fleet` (deferred orchestration). **Status:** Concept / Inception artifacts. Construction is gated on phase 1 validation (see `05-phase-plan.md`). | # | Doc | Purpose | |--
